site.name: Insight Chronicles
site.description: Independent long-form analysis on history, geopolitics, and technology.

articles.title: ULI: India's Attempt at a UPI Moment for Credit
articles.desc: The Reserve Bank's Unified Lending Interface links land records, GST data and credit bureaus into one API layer for lenders — 64 institutions have joined so far, but two unglamorous bottlenecks are slowing it down.
articles.content_html:
UPI solved how money moves. ONDC is attempting to solve how commerce is discovered and transacted. Account Aggregators and OCEN built a consent-based rail for sharing financial data with lenders. The Unified Lending Interface (ULI) is the Reserve Bank of India's attempt to close that loop — plugging shared financial data, along with land records, GST filings and credit bureau scores, directly into a lender's own loan-decisioning systems through one standardised set of APIs. Where OCEN made it possible to build a lending marketplace on top of shared data, ULI is the plumbing the RBI wants every regulated lender to plug into by default.

1) A single pipe for a lender's messiest problem

ULI, built by the Reserve Bank Innovation Hub (RBIH) in Bengaluru and piloted from August 2023, is not a lending product or a consumer-facing app. It is an interoperable API layer sitting between a regulated lender and the scattered data sources a credit decision actually depends on: land records held by state revenue departments, GST returns, income-tax filings, banking data through the Account Aggregator network, dairy-cooperative records for milk-based income, and traditional credit bureau scores.

Before ULI, a bank lending against a farmer's land needed a bespoke integration with that state's land-records system — many of which are not fully digitised to begin with. ULI's pitch is standardisation: one set of APIs and one consent flow, so any participating lender can pull verified data from any connected source in close to real time, instead of every institution separately negotiating access.

2) The fourth rail in a familiar pattern

UPI standardised payments. ONDC is attempting to unbundle commerce discovery from any single platform. Account Aggregators and OCEN built consent-based data sharing and a lending protocol on top of it. ULI extends that logic one layer further: instead of just moving data between institutions, it standardises how a lender's underwriting systems consume that data alongside government-held records — land, tax, cooperative — that were never part of the formal financial system's data pool before.

It is the same institutional instinct behind the rest of India Stack: build a public API layer, let banks, NBFCs and fintechs compete on top of it, and avoid any single app or vendor owning the interface itself.

3) The numbers so far

As of 12 December 2025, 64 lenders — 41 banks and 23 non-banking financial companies — were onboarded to ULI, up from 36 a year earlier, according to figures reported by Medianama in January 2026. Data services connected to the platform grew from roughly 50 to more than 136 over the same period, spanning 12 distinct loan "journeys." The flagship ones are Kisan Credit Card renewals, dairy-loan disbursement based on cooperative milk-pooling records, and MSME loans underwritten on GST and banking data rather than collateral.

Early pilot participants included Axis Bank, Federal Bank, Union Bank of India and lending-tech firm FinBox. By January 2026, the Department of Financial Services had convened a separate push to bring urban co-operative banks — including SVC Co-operative Bank, TJSB Sahakari Bank and Janata Sahakari Bank (Pune) — onto ULI, echoing how UPI's later growth came from expanding beyond the first wave of large banks.

4) Where scale-up is actually stalling

Two structural bottlenecks recur in reporting on ULI's rollout through 2025. First, large banks with existing proprietary lending stacks and fintech partnerships have limited incentive to re-plumb around a shared standard that duplicates systems already working for them — a version of the same private-rail-versus-public-rail tension that slowed early UPI adoption among bigger players. Second, land records in many states remain incomplete or not digitised to a standard ULI can consume, which directly undercuts one of its headline use cases: collateral-backed agricultural credit.

5) The risk the RBI itself has flagged

Standardising data access does not, on its own, make an underwriting decision safer. The RBI has acknowledged that the platform's early emphasis on small-ticket personal loans drew scrutiny as delinquencies in that category rose. Faster data assembly can make weak underwriting faster too, not only good underwriting — a distinction that matters more as the network scales into Kisan Credit Card and MSME lending, where borrowers are typically more collateral-poor and more exposed to income shocks than the urban personal-loan segment ULI first tested on.

6) Who is accountable when something goes wrong

In a response to an RTI request, the RBI stated that it does not itself manage consent boundaries, data-use limits, or borrower grievances at the platform level — that responsibility sits with the individual regulated lenders and data providers connected to ULI, not with a central grievance-redress body for the network as a whole. For a system explicitly designed to move sensitive land, income and credit data across institutions at scale, that is a meaningful gap. It puts ULI in a familiar position: fast institutional rollout, with the accountability architecture still catching up.

ULI's own numbers suggest the platform has crossed from pilot into something closer to durable infrastructure — 64 lenders and 136 data services is a meaningfully different scale than the 2023 trial. Whether it becomes as consequential as UPI probably depends less on further lender onboarding and more on two slower-moving inputs no API can standardise away: how fast states actually digitise land records, and whether the grievance and accountability layer catches up before — rather than after — lending volumes running through it get large enough for a bad underwriting call to matter at scale.

articles.title: Account Aggregators and OCEN: India's Third Rail After UPI and ONDC
articles.desc: After building open rails for payments and commerce, India built one for financial data and credit — the Account Aggregator framework and the Open Credit Enablement Network.
articles.content_html:
UPI solved how money moves. ONDC is attempting to solve how commerce is discovered and transacted. There's a third, less visible layer that determines something arguably more consequential for economic mobility: who gets access to credit, and on what terms. India's answer to that problem is the Account Aggregator (AA) framework, paired with the Open Credit Enablement Network (OCEN) — together they form a rail for financial data and lending that follows the same open-infrastructure logic as the rest of the India Stack.

1) The problem: credit access without collateral or history

A large share of small businesses and individuals in India are effectively invisible to formal lenders — not because they're bad credit risks, but because banks have no clean, verifiable way to see their income and cash flow. A small shopkeeper might have years of consistent revenue running through a bank account, UPI transactions, and GST filings, but without a way to package and share that data with a lender, none of it counts as "credit history" in the traditional sense. The result is that formal credit skews toward people who already have collateral or a documented financial past, while everyone else is pushed toward informal, often expensive, lending.

2) The Account Aggregator framework: consent as infrastructure

The Account Aggregator system, licensed and regulated by the Reserve Bank of India, is built around a simple idea: a person or business should be able to consent, digitally and specifically, to share their financial data — bank statements, GST returns, tax filings, insurance and investment records — from one institution directly to another, without the data ever passing through the Account Aggregator itself. The AA is a consent broker, not a data warehouse; it never sees the underlying financial data. The user picks exactly what to share, with whom, for what purpose, and for how long, and that consent is logged and revocable.

This is the same design instinct that shows up elsewhere in Indian digital infrastructure — separate the pipe from the data, keep the individual in control of the exchange, and let the ecosystem of banks, NBFCs, and fintechs build on top of a shared, auditable consent layer rather than each negotiating bilateral data-sharing deals.

3) OCEN: turning shared data into a lending protocol

If the Account Aggregator framework is the pipe for financial data, the Open Credit Enablement Network is the protocol for turning that data into an actual loan. OCEN standardises how a Loan Service Provider — which could be an e-commerce platform, a payments app, or any business with a relationship to a borrower — can plug a borrower's request into multiple lenders' systems, using AA-sourced data to underwrite the loan quickly. The practical effect is that a business you already use for something else (a marketplace, an accounting app, a payments app) can become the front end for a working-capital loan, sourced from a bank or NBFC you may never interact with directly, underwritten largely on the strength of your real transaction history rather than collateral or a lengthy paper application.

4) What this changes for small businesses

For a small manufacturer, retailer, or service provider, the practical shift is speed and access: working-capital credit that used to require weeks of paperwork and often a personal guarantee can, in principle, be underwritten in days using verified cash-flow data the business already generates. For lenders, it lowers the cost of underwriting thin-file borrowers, because the alternative to AA/OCEN-style data sharing is either declining the loan outright or pricing it high enough to cover the uncertainty. Cheaper, faster underwriting doesn't guarantee cheaper loans for borrowers, but it removes one of the largest structural reasons formal credit has historically excluded them.

5) The open questions

Consent-based data sharing is only as good as how well ordinary users understand what they're consenting to — a real risk is that consent screens become another form of fine print people click through without reading. Adoption also depends on enough banks, NBFCs, and data-holding institutions actually joining the AA network as Financial Information Providers; a consent framework with too few participating institutions doesn't unlock much. And because OCEN and AA route sensitive financial data between private and public institutions, the security and governance of the consent layer itself has to hold up under real adversarial pressure, not just in a controlled pilot.

Why it matters

Put together, UPI, ONDC, and the AA/OCEN stack cover the three things a functioning market economy needs at scale: a way to move money, a way to discover and transact goods and services, and a way to extend credit based on real financial behaviour rather than proxies like collateral or a name on an approved list. Whether or not any single piece fully lives up to its design intent, the pattern is now a recognisable playbook — and other countries studying India's digital infrastructure are watching this credit layer as closely as they watched UPI.

articles.title: GeM: How India Turned Government Procurement Into Open Infrastructure
articles.desc: Government e-Marketplace (GeM) rebuilt how India's public sector buys goods and services — turning a slow, opaque tendering process into an open, competitive digital marketplace.
articles.content_html:
Government procurement is one of the least glamorous parts of public administration, and one of the most consequential. Every year, central and state government departments, public sector undertakings, and autonomous bodies buy an enormous volume of goods and services — office supplies, IT equipment, vehicles, construction materials, consulting work. For decades in India, most of that buying happened through a patchwork of paper tenders, department-specific rate contracts, and manual approval chains. It was slow, hard to audit, and structurally friendly to insiders who understood how to navigate it.

The Government e-Marketplace, launched in 2016, set out to change that by treating procurement itself as infrastructure — a shared, digital, rules-based system that any eligible seller could plug into, and any government buyer could use without reinventing the process each time.

1) The problem GeM was built to solve

Before GeM, a government office that needed, say, 50 laptops would typically issue a tender, wait for physical bids, evaluate them manually, and negotiate a rate — a process that could take weeks or months for a routine purchase. Because each department ran its own process, prices for identical items varied wildly across ministries, and there was no easy way to compare what one office paid against another. That opacity wasn't necessarily always corrupt, but it made corruption easy to hide inside, and it made honest price discovery nearly impossible.

2) The design: a marketplace, not a portal

The distinction matters. Many government digitisation efforts amount to putting a paper form online — a portal. GeM was designed as an actual marketplace: sellers list products and services with prices, buyers compare listings, and standard categories exist so a laptop from one vendor can be compared against a laptop from another on roughly equal terms. Reverse auctions and demand aggregation let government buyers pool purchasing power the way a large private enterprise would, pushing prices down. Every transaction leaves a digital trail — what was bought, from whom, at what price, approved by whom — which is a meaningful upgrade for auditability even if it doesn't eliminate all forms of gaming.

3) Who benefits, and how much

The most consequential shift may be on the seller side. Traditional government tendering favoured large, well-resourced vendors who could handle the paperwork and relationships. GeM's standardised, online listing process lowered that barrier considerably, opening the door for small and medium enterprises, and in some categories for individual artisans and self-help groups, to sell directly to government buyers without an intermediary. That's a meaningfully different distribution of opportunity than the tendering system it replaced, even though large vendors still do substantial volume on the platform.

On the buyer side, government departments get faster procurement cycles, comparable pricing across categories, and — because everything is logged — a much easier basis for internal and external audit.

4) The limits

GeM is not a cure-all. Marketplaces are only as good as the competition within them — if too few sellers list in a given category, price discovery weakens. Quality assurance is a persistent challenge: a photo and a listed spec sheet are a weaker signal than an in-person vendor evaluation, and complaints about mismatched quality do surface. And digitising a process doesn't automatically digitise the incentives behind it — departments under pressure to spend a budget by a fiscal year-end can still make rushed purchasing decisions, just now through a faster interface. GeM reduces certain categories of friction and opacity; it doesn't by itself guarantee that every purchase is optimal.

5) Why this fits the larger India Stack pattern

GeM follows the same institutional logic as UPI for payments or ONDC for commerce: build a shared, open, rules-based rail rather than a bespoke system for each participant, and let competition happen on top of it rather than around it. The pattern recurs across Indian public infrastructure because it solves a specific problem well — coordinating a very large number of participants (thousands of government buying entities, lakhs of sellers) without requiring any single actor to build bilateral relationships with every other one. Procurement was a natural, if unglamorous, candidate for the same treatment, and the results — faster cycles, wider seller access, and an auditable record — suggest the model generalises beyond payments and identity into the more mundane, high-volume machinery of the state.
